title: Measurements of Gamow-Teller transitions from 59Co via the 59Co(t,3He+gamma) charge-exchange reaction and its application to the stellar electron- capture rates

x4freetext: Measurements of Gamow-Teller transitions from 59Co
via the 59Co(t,3He+gamma) charge-exchange reaction
and its application to the stellar electron- capture
rates

x4freetext: The Coupled Cyclotron Facility (CCF)
at the National Superconducting Cyclotron Laboratory
(NSCL).
An 16O primary beam was accelerated by the CCF to an
energy of 150 MeV/u with an intensity of ~150 pnA.
The 16O beam impinged on a beryllium target with a
thickness of 3525 mg/cm2 to produce a secondary
triton beam via fragmentation reactions. The A1900
fragment separator was used to select the tritons
from other fragmentation products using magnetic
rigidity and energy-loss (Brho-dE-Brho) selections.
The produced triton beam had an energy of 115 MeV/u
and an intensity of ~4*10**6 particles per second.
The detection system includes
two position sensitive cathode readout drift chambers
(CRDCs) and a 5-mm-thick plastic scintillator. The
hit positions and track angles of the ejectiles at
the focal plane were measured by the CRDCs. The
plastic scintillator measured energy loss (dE) and,
together with the radio-frequency signal from the
CCF, time-of-flight (TOF) of the ejectiles. The 3He
particles are then clearly identified in the TOF-dE
two dimensional plot. The Gamma-Ray Energy Tracking
In-beam Nuclear Array (GRETINA) was placed around the
59Co target to detect the deexcitation gamma rays
from the 59Fe residual nucleus or its decay products
after neutron and/or proton emission. For this
experiment, GRETINA consisted of 12 detector modules
covering more than 1pi solid angles.
The 59Co target used for the experiment had a
thickness of 22.25 mg/cm2. A polyethylene (CH2) target
with a thickness of 4.1 mg/cm2 was also used at the
beginning of the experiment for calibration purposes
and for tuning the dispersion-matching conditions of
the spectrometer.
Differential cross sections for the
charge-exchange reaction were measured using the S800
spectrometer. To resolve individual levels that are
not distinguishable in the S800 particle singles data,
coincident gamma rays from the 59Fe residual nucleus
were detected by using the Gamma-Ray Energy Tracking.
Statistical uncertainties.
Approved by B.Gao on December 19, 2025.
BP
